quiz.title: Unit 7 Quiz 6, Biology I
quiz.subtitle: Cell Division: Meiosis and Genetic Variation
quiz.subject: Biology
quiz.topic: Meiosis Stages, Crossing Over, Independent Assortment, Tetrads, Homologous vs Sister Chromatid Separation, Gamete Production

questions.question: What is the main purpose of meiosis?
questions.explanation: The main purpose of meiosis is to produce gametes (sperm and egg cells) for sexual reproduction. Mitosis is responsible for growth, tissue repair, and asexual reproduction.

questions.question: How many nuclear divisions occur during meiosis?
questions.explanation: Meiosis involves two nuclear divisions: meiosis I (reductional division) and meiosis II (equational division). This produces four haploid daughter cells from one diploid parent cell.

questions.question: During which phase does crossing over occur?
questions.explanation: Crossing over occurs during pachytene of prophase I, when homologous chromosomes are fully paired in tetrads. The synaptonemal complex facilitates the exchange of genetic material between nonsister chromatids.

questions.question: Homologous chromosomes separate during:
questions.explanation: Homologous chromosomes separate during anaphase I of meiosis, reducing the chromosome number by half. Sister chromatids remain attached until anaphase II.

questions.question: Sister chromatids separate during:
questions.explanation: Sister chromatids separate during anaphase II of meiosis, similar to mitosis. Anaphase I separates homologous chromosomes, not sister chromatids.

questions.question: Select all that apply: Which of the following occurs only in meiosis I, not meiosis II?
questions.explanation: Tetrad formation (synapsis of homologous chromosomes) and crossing over occur only in prophase I of meiosis. Separation of sister chromatids occurs in anaphase II, cytokinesis occurs after both divisions, and chromosome condensation occurs in both meiosis I and II.

questions.question: At the end of meiosis II, each gamete has:
options: The same number of chromosomes as the parent cell
Half the chromosome number of the parent cell
Twice the chromosome number of the parent cell
One-third the chromosome number of the parent cell
questions.explanation: At the end of meiosis II, each gamete is haploid, containing half the chromosome number of the original diploid parent cell. This ensures chromosome number is maintained during fertilization.

questions.question: How many daughter cells are produced at the end of meiosis?
questions.explanation: Meiosis produces four genetically unique haploid daughter cells from one diploid parent cell. Mitosis produces two identical daughter cells.

questions.question: Select all that apply: Meiosis contributes to genetic variation through:
questions.explanation: Genetic variation in meiosis comes from two main sources: crossing over (recombination during prophase I) and independent assortment of homologous chromosomes during metaphase I. DNA replication, cytokinesis, and chromosome condensation do not directly create genetic variation.

questions.question: Tetrads are formed during:
questions.explanation: Tetrads (groups of four chromatids from paired homologous chromosomes) form during prophase I of meiosis when synapsis occurs. This allows for crossing over between nonsister chromatids.

questions.question: Independent assortment occurs during:
questions.explanation: Independent assortment occurs during metaphase I when homologous chromosome pairs align randomly at the metaphase plate. The random orientation determines which chromosomes go to which daughter cells.

questions.question: How does meiosis II differ from mitosis?
options: Meiosis II occurs without prior DNA replication
questions.explanation: Meiosis II differs from mitosis because it occurs without prior DNA replication. Cells enter meiosis II directly after meiosis I and cytokinesis, with no S phase in between. Both separate sister chromatids.

questions.question: Crossing over occurs between:
options: Sister chromatids of the same chromosome
questions.explanation: Crossing over occurs between nonsister chromatids of homologous chromosomes during prophase I. This exchange of genetic material creates new combinations of alleles.

questions.question: Which phase marks the end of meiosis I?
questions.explanation: Telophase I marks the end of meiosis I, followed by cytokinesis. The cell then enters a brief interkinesis (often without DNA replication) before meiosis II begins.

questions.question: Which of the following statements is true?
options: Meiosis produces gametes with the same chromosome number as the parent
Meiosis occurs only in somatic cells
questions.explanation: Meiosis introduces genetic variation through crossing over and independent assortment. It produces genetically unique haploid gametes, occurs in germ cells (not somatic cells), and halves chromosome number.

scoreMessages.90: Outstanding! You've mastered the complexities of meiosis! 🧬
scoreMessages.70: Good work! Review the differences between meiosis I and meiosis II.
scoreMessages.50: Satisfactory. Focus on crossing over and independent assortment.
scoreMessages.0: Please review homologous chromosome separation, tetrads, and gamete production.
